name.text: Calculating the perimeter of the original and image triangle after the enlargement.

questiontext.text:
A right-angled triangle has side lengths of \({@base@}\) cm, \({@height@}\) cm, and \({@hypotenuse@}\) cm.

This triangle undergoes an enlargement with a scale factor of \(k = {@sf@}\).

  1. Calculate the perimeter of the original triangle.
    [[input:ans1]] cm[[validation:ans1]][[feedback:prt1]]
  2. Calculate the perimeter of the image triangle after the enlargement.
    [[input:ans2]] cm [[validation:ans2]][[feedback:prt2]]

generalfeedback.text:
Worked Solution

Step 1: Perimeter of the Original Triangle

The perimeter is the sum of all side lengths:

\(\begin{align} P_{\text{original}} &= {@base@} + {@height@} + {@hypotenuse@} \\ &= \mathbf{{@ta1@} \text{ cm}} \end{align}\) Step 2: Understanding the Effect of a Negative Scale Factor

A negative scale factor, such as \(k = {@sf@}\), indicates that the image is inverted and on the opposite side of the center of enlargement. However, physical lengths cannot be negative.

When calculating the dimensions of the image, we multiply the original lengths by the absolute value of the scale factor (\(|k|\)):

\[ \text{Image Length} = \text{Original Length} \times |k| \] Step 3: Perimeter of the Image

We apply the scale factor to the original perimeter:

\(\begin{align} P_{\text{image}} &= P_{\text{original}} \times |{@sf@}| \\ &= {@ta1@} \times {@abs(sf)@} \\ &= {@ta2@} \text{ cm} \end{align}\)

prtcorrect.text: Correct answer, well done.

prtpartiallycorrect.text: Your answer is partially correct.

prtincorrect.text: Incorrect answer.
